cards.prompt: A base-class constraint where T : Employee unlocks e.Name (without it the compiler can only assume System.Object members, CS1061); Manager derives from Employee and passes the gate, so NameOf(new Manager("Ann")) prints Ann
cards.code: using System; static string NameOf<T>(T e) where T : Employee => e.Name; Console.WriteLine(NameOf(new Manager("Ann"))); class Employee { public string Name; public Employee(string n){Name=n;} } class Manager : Employee { public Manager(string n):base(n){} }

cards.prompt: The unmanaged constraint lets sizeof(T) work in an unsafe context (types manipulated as blocks of memory); int is 4, double is 8, Guid is 16 bytes
cards.code: using System; unsafe static int Size<T>() where T : unmanaged => sizeof(T); Console.WriteLine($"{Size<int>()} {Size<double>()} {Size<Guid>()}");

cards.prompt: An interface constraint where T : IComparable<T> unlocks a.CompareTo(b); int and string both implement it, so Max(3,9) is 9 and Max("apple","pear") is pear (lexicographically greater)
cards.code: using System; static T Max<T>(T a, T b) where T : IComparable<T> => a.CompareTo(b) >= 0 ? a : b; Console.WriteLine($"{Max(3, 9)} {Max("apple", "pear")}");
